papers.title: The Nearest High-Velocity Stars Revealed by LAMOST Data Release 1
papers.author_names: Jing Zhong Li Chen Chao Liu Richard de Grijs Jinliang Hou Shiyin Shen Zhengyi Shao Jing Li Ali Luo Jianrong Shi Haotong Zhang Ming Yang Licai Deng Ge Jin Yong Zhang Yonghui Hou Zhenchao Zhang

abstract.text: We report the discovery of 28 candidate high-velocity stars (HVSs) at heliocentric distances of less than 3 kpc, based on the Large Sky Area Multi-Object Fiber Spectroscopic Telescope (LAMOST) Data Release 1. Our sample of HVS candidates covers a much broader color range than the equivalent ranges discussed in previous studies and comprises the first and largest sample of HVSs in the solar neighborhood. The sufficiently accurate observed and derived parameters for all candidates allow us to ascertain their nature as genuine HVSs, while a subset of 12 objects represents the most promising candidates. Our results also highlight the great potential of discovering statistically large numbers of HVSs of different spectral types in LAMOST survey data. This will ultimately enable us to achieve a better understanding of the nature of Galactic HVSs and their ejection mechanisms, and to constrain the structure of the Galaxy.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] LAMOST Data Release 1 enables the identification of 28 high-velocity star (HVS) candidates within 3 kpc of the Sun, with 12 exceeding 400 km/s, representing the first large, statistically robust sample of HVSs in the solar neighborhood. The sample spans a broader spectral and color range than prior studies, including F/G/K-type stars, and provides critical data to test ejection mechanisms such as three-body interactions and supermassive black hole encounters by probing the kinematics and diversity of low-distance HVSs.

source_fields.title: The Nearest High-Velocity Stars Revealed by LAMOST Data Release 1

source_fields.sections_text: Conclusions: This study presents the first large, diverse sample of high-velocity star (HVS) candidates in the solar neighborhood, validated through multi-catalog proper motion comparisons, spectral analysis, and velocity calculations, highlighting the potential of LAMOST data for systematic HVS discovery and Galactic structure studies. Introduction: This paper presents the first large sample of hypervelocity star candidates in the solar neighborhood, significantly expanding the spectral and distance range of known HVSs and enabling a more comprehensive test of ejection mechanisms beyond previous studies focused on distant, early-type stars. HVS Candidate Verification: The section verifies the radial velocity measurements of LAMOST high-velocity star candidates using IRAF, demonstrating higher accuracy and consistency compared to the LAMOST 1D pipeline and showing agreement with SDSS and literature values, including a well-known hyper-runaway star. LAMOST Survey Data and HVS Candidate Selection: This section describes the selection of high-velocity star (HVS) candidates from LAMOST DR1 data by applying stringent criteria on radial velocities, proper motions, photometry, metallicities, and space velocities to identify a final sample of approximately 200 stars with Galactic rest velocities exceeding 300 km s⁻¹.

abstract: We report the discovery of 28 candidate high-velocity stars (HVSs) at heliocentric distances of less than 3 kpc, based on the Large Sky Area Multi-Object Fiber Spectroscopic Telescope (LAMOST) Data Release
title: The Nearest High-Velocity Stars Revealed by LAMOST Data Release 1

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title and abstract explicitly state discovery of 28 candidate high-velocity stars from LAMOST Data Release 1, with derived parameters and candidate lists.

papers.title: Predicted Space Motions for Hypervelocity and Runaway Stars: Proper Motions and Radial Velocities for the GAIA Era
papers.author_names: Scott J. Kenyon Benjamin C. Bromley Warren R. Brown Margaret J. Geller

abstract.text: We predict the distinctive three dimensional space motions of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) and runaway stars moving in a realistic Galactic potential. For nearby stars with distances less than 10~kpc, unbound stars are rare; proper motions alone rarely isolate bound HVSs and runaways from indigenous halo stars. At large distances of 20-100 kpc, unbound HVSs are much more common than runaways; radial velocities easily distinguish both from indigenous halo stars. Comparisons of the predictions with existing observations are encouraging. Although the models fail to match observations of solar-type HVS candidates from SEGUE, they agree well with data for B-type HVS and runaways from other surveys. Complete samples of g <= 20 stars with GAIA should provide clear tests of formation models for HVSs and runaways and will enable accurate probes of the shape of the Galactic potential.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper develops analytic and numerical models of three-dimensional space motions for hypervelocity stars (HVSs) and runaway stars in a realistic Galactic potential, showing that radial velocities effectively distinguish unbound stars from indigenous halo stars at distances >20 kpc, while proper motions offer limited discrimination near the Galactic center. The models reveal distinct velocity patterns by Galactic longitude/latitude, enabling identification of HVSs and runaways, with strong evidence favoring HVS origins among distant high-velocity stars—particularly B-type stars—suggesting that GAIA's full sample of g<20 stars will provide critical tests of Galactic formation models and potential anisotropy.

source_fields.title: Predicted Space Motions for Hypervelocity and Runaway Stars: Proper Motions and Radial Velocities for the GAIA Era

source_fields.sections_text: OVERVIEW: This section outlines both analytic models and numerical simulations to predict the proper motions and radial velocities of hypervelocity and runaway stars, emphasizing how these motions can be used to distinguish ejected stars from other stellar populations based on distance and velocity characteristics. INTRODUCTION: This paper introduces a novel method using proper motion and radial velocity variations in an axisymmetric Galactic potential to robustly distinguish between high-velocity stars originating from the Galactic Center (HVSs) and dynamical runaways, enabling more accurate constraints on the Milky Way's mass and potential anisotropy. ANALYTIC MODEL: The paper proposes a coordinate transformation method that maps stellar positions and velocities from a Galactic center (GC) reference frame to a heliocentric galactic frame, enabling accurate calculation of radial, tangential, and proper motions while accounting for the Sun's position and motion and resolving angular degeneracies in galactic longitude. REACHING THE HALO: Ejected stars can reach the Milky Way's halo if their initial velocity exceeds the local escape velocity, with dynamical ejections and Galactic rotation playing key roles in enabling escape, particularly for stars ejected in the direction of Galactic rotation. OBSERVATIONAL TESTS: Observational tests using samples of ejected stars from SDSS, including HVSs, runaways, and high-velocity main sequence stars, aim to distinguish between models by comparing their predicted radial velocities, proper motions, distances, and velocity dispersions with observed data. NUMERICAL SIMULATIONS: This section describes numerical simulations of hypervelocity stars and runaways in a realistic Galactic potential, focusing on their observable properties in the heliocentric frame through detailed orbital integrations and initial condition modeling. DISCUSSION AND SUMMARY: Simulations show that HVSs have higher space velocities than runaways, with unbound HVSs dominating at large distances and bound HVSs/runaways being more common nearby, where radial velocity and proper motion distributions—especially double-peaked proper motions at 20–50 kpc and GC-associated concentrations—clearly distinguish ejected stars from halo stars, and these trends are robust across modeling assumptions and observational data. COMPLETE SAMPLES OF STARS: This section presents numerical simulations of bound and unbound stars ejected from the Galactic center, providing predicted velocity and proper motion distributions for hypervelocity and runaway stars that can be observed with GAIA and large ground-based telescopes. DISTANCE LIMITED SAMPLES OF STARS: This section presents predictions for the radial velocity and proper motion distributions of distance-limited samples of 1 and 3 $M_{\\odot}$ hypervelocity and runaway stars, based on GAIA-sensitivity magnitude limits and stellar evolution models, showing distinct spatial and kinematic characteristics between HVSs and runaways.

abstract: We predict the distinctive three dimensional space motions of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) and runaway stars moving in a realistic Galactic potential.
For nearby stars with distances less than 10~kpc, unbound stars are rare; proper motions alone rarely isolate bound HVSs and runaways from indigenous halo stars.
At large distances of 20-100 kpc, unbound HVSs are much more common than runaways; radial velocities easily distinguish both from indigenous halo stars.
Complete samples of g <= 20 stars with GAIA should provide clear tests of formation models for HVSs and runaways and will enable accurate probes of the shape of the Galactic potential.
title: Predicted Space Motions for Hypervelocity and Runaway Stars: Proper Motions and Radial Velocities for

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents theoretical models and predictions for space motions of hypervelocity and runaway stars; mentions comparisons with existing observations but does not present new observational data or catalog.

papers.title: Escape fraction of ionizing photons during reionization: effects due to supernova feedback and runaway OB stars

abstract.text: The fraction of hydrogen ionizing photons escaping from galaxies into the intergalactic medium is a critical ingredient in the theory of reionization. We use two zoomed-in, high-resolution (4 pc), cosmological radiation hydrodynamic simulations with adaptive mesh refinement to investigate the impact of two physical mechanisms (supernova feedback and runaway OB stars) on the escape fraction (f_esc) at the epoch of reionization (z>7). We implement a new, physically motivated supernova feedback model that can approximate the Sedov solutions at all (from the free expansion to snowplow) stages. We find that there is a significant time delay of about ten million years between the peak of star formation and that of escape fraction, due to the time required for the build-up and subsequent destruction of the star-forming cloud by supernova feedback. Consequently, the photon number-weighted mean escape fraction for dwarf galaxies in halos of mass 10^8-10^10.5 Msun is found to be <fesc>~11%, although instantaneous values of f_esc>20% are common when star formation is strongly modulated by the supernova explosions. We find that the inclusion of runaway OB stars increases the mean escape fraction by 22% to <fesc>~14%. As supernovae resulting from runaway OB stars tend to occur in less dense environments, the feedback effect is enhanced and star formation is further suppressed in halos with Mvir>10^9 Msun in the simulation with runaway OB stars compared with the model without them. While both our models produce enough ionizing photons to maintain a fully ionized universe at z>7 as observed, a still higher amount of ionizing photons at z>9 appears necessary to accommodate the high observed electron optical depth inferred from cosmic microwave background observations.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Explicitly studies runaway OB stars as a mechanism affecting escape fraction of ionizing photons during reionization

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: 2. Method: The paper proposes a cosmological hydrodynamics and radiative transfer simulation using the RAMSES code with multi-group radiation transfer to model the escape of ionizing radiation from high-redshift galaxies, leveraging adaptive mesh refinement, metal cooling, and a Kroupa initial mass function to track photoionization and heating in realistic halo environments. 3. Results: The experiment demonstrates that the physically motivated SN feedback model significantly improves baryon-to-star conversion efficiency at $z=7$, with central galaxies achieving a baryon-to-star conversion efficiency exceeding 50% of the cosmic mean and a median escape fraction of ionizing photons above 10% in massive halos, outperforming previous thermal or kinetic feedback schemes. 4. Discussion: The experiment finds that the FRU model yields a Thomson optical depth of $\\tau_e = 0.065$, consistent only within 2$\\sigma$ with CMB measurements, indicating a need for a higher escape fraction of ionizing photons—specifically, $Q_{\\rm HII} \\gtrsim 20\\%$ at $z \\sim 10$—to achieve sufficient reionization by $z = 7.25$. 5. Conclusions: The escape fraction of ionizing photons is strongly regulated by supernova feedback and delayed star formation, with only a small fraction of photons escaping despite high instantaneous escape rates, and the overall escape probability being more dependent on the timing of feedback than on halo mass or redshift. 1. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel approach to estimating the escape fraction of ionizing photons from dwarf galaxies by leveraging the observed cosmic microwave background polarization and quasar Lyα forest data, thereby addressing the key uncertainty in stellar reionization models regarding photon escape in the early universe. Appendix C Effect of spatial resolution: Varying spatial resolution affects the mean escape fraction of ionizing photons, with lower resolution leading to a reduced escape fraction due to slower star formation and delayed collapse, indicating convergence in the fiducial run. Appendix A A New Physical Scheme For Supernova Feedback: This section proposes a new physical scheme for supernova feedback that addresses the issue of excessive cooling by accurately modeling the momentum transfer from SN explosions through the Sedov phases, ensuring a physically consistent deposition of energy and momentum that is robust across different simulation resolutions. Appendix B Suppression of star formation in low-mass halos: Supernova feedback, not photoionizing radiation, is the primary cause of the low star formation efficiency in low-mass halos.

We find that the inclusion of runaway OB stars increases the mean escape fraction by 22% to ~14%.

As supernovae resulting from runaway OB stars tend to occur in less dense environments, the feedback effect is enhanced and star formation is further suppressed in halos with Mvir>10^9 Msun in the simulation
with runaway OB stars compared with the model without them.
